Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Principle The Well Ordering Principle operates on three fundamental pillars: - **Existence of a Set:** $S$ must be a clearly defined, non-empty set of positive integers. - **Order Relation:** A binary relation $\leq$ that is reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive (a total order on $S$). - **Minimality Guarantee:** The set $S$ is guaranteed to contain a unique least element $m$ satisfying $m \leq s$ for every $s \in S$. ### 2.2 Why the Well Ordering Principle Works — The "Why" Behind It > [!IMPORTANT] > The WOP is one of the **five Peano axioms** that formally define the natural numbers. It is logically equivalent to the **Principle of Mathematical Induction**. This equivalence is a high-yield KTU concept: > - **WOP $\Rightarrow$ Induction** > - **Induction $\Rightarrow$ WOP** > - **WOP $\Rightarrow$ Strong Induction** > - **WOP $\Rightarrow$ Euclidean Algorithm terminates** ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Property / Theorem** | **Mathematical Statement** | **Conditions** | **Application** | |---|---|---|---| | Well Ordering Principle | $\forall S \subseteq \mathbb{N}, S \neq \emptyset \Rightarrow \exists m \in S \text{ such that } m \leq s \; \forall s \in S$ | $S$ non-empty, $S \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ | Proof by contradiction of minimal counterexamples | | Equivalence with Induction | $P(1) \land \forall n \in \mathbb{N}, [P(n) \Rightarrow P(n+1)] \Rightarrow \forall n \in \mathbb{N}, P(n)$ | Base case + inductive step | Proving propositions about $\mathbb{N}$ | | Archimedean Property | $\forall x, y \in \mathbb{R}^+, \exists n \in \mathbb{N}: nx > y$ | Positive reals | Bounding quantities in security proofs | | Minimal Counterexample | If $S$ is non-empty, $\exists m \in S$ with $m = \min(S)$ | Used in proof by contradiction | Cryptographic proof techniques | | Well-Ordered Set Definition | $\forall T \subseteq S, T \neq \emptyset \Rightarrow T \text{ has a least element}$ | Total order on $S$ | Set theory foundations | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility in Security:** The WOP underpins **proofs of termination** for cryptographic algorithms (e.g., RSA key generation, Euclidean algorithm for modular inverse), **proofs of correctness** for security protocols, and **proofs of soundness** for access control models. ### 2.4 Set-Theoretic Foundation For any non-empty set $S \subseteq \mathbb{N}$, define: $$m = \min(S) \iff m \in S \land \forall s \in S, m \leq s$$ The element $m$ is **unique** because if both $m_1$ and $m_2$ are minima, then $m_1 \leq m_2$ and $m_2 \leq m_1$, so by antisymmetry $m_1 = m_2$. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Proof of Equivalence: WOP $\iff$ Mathematical Induction > [!IMPORTANT] > This is a frequently tested KTU proof — master every step. #### 3.1.1 Proof: WOP $\Rightarrow$ Mathematical Induction **Statement:** If WOP holds, then for any predicate $P(n)$ defined on $\mathbb{N}$: $$P(1) \land [\forall n \in \mathbb{N}, P(n) \Rightarrow P(n+1)] \Rightarrow \forall n \in \mathbb{N}, P(n)$$ **Step 1 — Assume the contrary:** Suppose $P(1)$ is true and $P(n) \Rightarrow P(n+1)$ holds for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$, but $P(n)$ is **false** for some $n \in \mathbb{N}$. **Step 2 — Define the counterexample set:** Let $$S = \{n \in \mathbb{N} \mid P(n) \text{ is false}\}$$ **Step 3 — Establish non-emptiness:** By our assumption in Step 1, $S$ is non-empty (there exists at least one $n$ for which $P(n)$ fails). **Step 4 — Apply the WOP:** Since $S \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ and $S \neq \emptyset$, by the Well Ordering Principle, $S$ has a least element. Call it $m = \min(S)$. **Step 5 — Derive a contradiction:** - Note that $m \neq 1$ because $P(1)$ is true (given assumption), so $1 \notin S$. - Therefore, $m - 1 \in \mathbb{N}$ and $m - 1 \notin S$, which means $P(m-1)$ is **true**. - By the inductive hypothesis, $P(m-1) \Rightarrow P(m)$, so $P(m)$ is **true**. - But $m \in S$ means $P(m)$ is **false** — **contradiction!** **Step 6 — Conclusion:** The assumption that $S$ is non-empty must be false. Therefore, $S = \emptyset$, meaning $P(n)$ holds for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$. $\blacksquare$ #### 3.1.2 Proof: Mathematical Induction $\Rightarrow$ WOP **Statement:** If induction holds, then every non-empty $S \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ has a least element. **Step 1 — Assume the contrary:** Suppose $S \subseteq \mathbb{N}$, $S \neq \emptyset$, but $S$ has **no least element**. **Step 2 — Construct a predicate:** Define $$P(n) = \text{"For all } k \in \mathbb{N} \text{ with } 1 \leq k \leq n, k \notin S"$$ **Step 3 — Verify the base case:** $P(1)$: If $1 \in S$, then $1$ would be the least element of $S$ (since $1$ is the smallest natural number). Since $S$ has no least element, $1 \notin S$. So $P(1)$ is **true**. **Step 4 — Verify the inductive step:** Suppose $P(n)$ is true. We need to show $P(n+1)$ is true. - $P(n)$ says: no integer from $1$ to $n$ is in $S$. - If $(n+1) \in S$, then $(n+1)$ is the smallest element of $S$ (since none of $1, 2, \ldots, n$ are in $S$). - But $S$ has no least element by assumption, so $(n+1) \notin S$. - Therefore, no integer from $1$ to $n+1$ is in $S$, so $P(n+1)$ is **true**. **Step 5 — Apply induction:** By mathematical induction, $P(n)$ is true for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$, meaning $S = \emptyset$. **Step 6 — Contradiction and conclusion:** This contradicts $S \neq \emptyset$. Therefore, $S$ must have a least element. $\blacksquare$ ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Counterexample to WOP on Non-Well-Ordered Sets | **Set** | **Well-Ordered?** | **Reason** | **Smallest Element** | |---|---|---|---| | $\mathbb{N} = \{1, 2, 3, \ldots\}$ | ✅ Yes | Every non-empty subset has a least element | $1$ | | $\mathbb{Z} = \{\ldots, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$ | ❌ No | $\mathbb{Z}$ itself is non-empty but has no least element | None | | $\mathbb{Q} \cap (0, 1)$ | ❌ No | For any $q \in S$, $q/2 \in S$ but $q/2 < q$ | None | | $\mathbb{R}^+ = (0, \infty)$ | ❌ No | No lower bound exists | None | | $\{1, 3, 5, 7, \ldots\}$ (odd positive integers) | ✅ Yes | Discrete jump structure preserves minimality | $1$ | | Finite subset of $\mathbb{N}$ | ✅ Yes | Any finite non-empty set has a smallest element | $\min(S)$ | | $\{1/n \mid n \in \mathbb{N}\}$ | ✅ Yes | Set is $\{1, 1/2, 1/3, \ldots\}$; minimum exists | $1/n \to 0$ but $0 \notin S$ | ### 3.3 Worked Example: Proof by WOP (Security Application) > [!NOTE] > **Theorem (Goldbach-type security claim):** Let $S = \{n \in \mathbb{N} \mid n > 1 \text{ and } n \text{ is not expressible as a product of primes}\}$. Prove that $S$ is empty. **Step 1 — Suppose $S$ is non-empty.** Then $S \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ and $S \neq \emptyset$. **Step 2 — By WOP, $S$ has a least element $m$.** **Step 3 — Analyze $m$:** Since $m \in S$, $m > 1$ and $m$ is not expressible as a product of primes. - If $m$ is prime, then $m = m$ (a product of one prime) — **contradiction**. - If $m$ is composite, then $m = ab$ for some $a, b \in \mathbb{N}$ with $1 < a, b < m$. - By well-ordering, both $a$ and $b$ are expressible as prime products, so $m$ is too — **contradiction**. **Step 4 — Conclusion:** $S$ must be empty, proving that every integer $> 1$ is a product of primes. $\blacksquare$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Logical Equivalence Map of WOP The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the logical equivalence between the Well Ordering Principle, Mathematical Induction, and Strong Induction, along with their applications in security proofs. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["Well Ordering Principle
Every non-empty subset of N
has a least element"] nodeB["Mathematical Induction
P1 and Pn implies Pn+1
then Pn for all n"] nodeC["Strong Induction
P1 and P1 through Pn
implies Pn+1, then Pn for all n"] nodeD["Euclidean Algorithm
Termination Proof"] nodeE["RSA Key Generation
Correctness Proof"] nodeF["Access Control Models
Soundness Verification"] nodeG["Cryptographic Hash
Collision Resistance Proof"] nodeA -->|"implies"| nodeB nodeB -->|"implies"| nodeA nodeA -->|"implies"| nodeC nodeC -->|"implies"| nodeA nodeA -->|"applied in"| nodeD nodeB -->|"applied in"| nodeE nodeC -->|"applied in"| nodeF nodeA -->|"applied in"| nodeG ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: WOP-Based Proof Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR input1["Hypothesis: S subset of N
S is non-empty"] step1["Apply WOP
Find m = min S"] step2["Analyze m
Case Analysis"] step3["Derive
Contradiction"] output1["Conclusion
S is empty"] input1 --> step1 step1 --> step2 step2 --> step3 step3 --> output1 ``` ### 4.3 Decision Tree: Is a Set Well-Ordered? ```mermaid flowchart TD start["Given Set S with order relation"] q1{"Is S a subset
of N?"} q2{"Is S non-empty?"} q3{"Does every
non-empty subset
of S have a
least element?"} res1["Not Applicable
WOP does not apply"] res2["S is empty
Vacuously well-ordered"] res3["S is well-ordered
WOP holds"] res4["S is NOT well-ordered
Counterexample exists"] start --> q1 q1 -->|"No"| res1 q1 -->|"Yes"| q2 q2 -->|"No"| res2 q2 -->|"Yes"| q3 q3 -->|"Yes"| res3 q3 -->|"No"| res4 ``` ### 4.4 Set Hierarchy Diagram ```mermaid graph TD reals["R (Real Numbers)
NOT well-ordered"] rationals["Q (Rationals)
NOT well-ordered"] integers["Z (Integers)
NOT well-ordered"] naturals["N (Natural Numbers)
WELL-ORDERED"] subset1["Any non-empty subset of N
WELL-ORDERED"] subset2["Finite subsets of N
WELL-ORDERED"] reals -->|"contains"| rationals rationals -->|"contains"| integers integers -->|"contains"| naturals naturals -->|"contains"| subset1 subset1 -->|"contains"| subset2 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **State and explain the Well Ordering Principle. Give one example and one counterexample.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Well Ordering Principle:** Every non-empty set of positive integers has a least element. Formally, if $S \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ and $S \neq \emptyset$, then there exists $m \in S$ such that $m \leq s$ for all $s \in S$. **Example (1 mark):** The set $S = \{5, 12, 3, 8, 1, 7\}$ is a non-empty subset of $\mathbb{N}$. Its least element is $\min(S) = 1$. **Counterexample (1 mark):** The set $T = \{x \in \mathbb{R} \mid x > 0\}$ is a non-empty set of positive real numbers, but it has no least element because for any candidate minimum $m > 0$, the number $m/2$ is also in $T$ and $m/2 < m$. Hence, $T$ is not well-ordered. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Distinguish between a well-ordered set and a partially ordered set with a suitable example.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | **Criterion** | **Well-Ordered Set** | **Partially Ordered Set** | |---|---|---| | Order Type | **Total order** (every pair comparable) | Partial order (some pairs may be incomparable) | | Least Element | **Guaranteed** for every non-empty subset | **Not guaranteed** | | Example | $\mathbb{N} = \{1, 2, 3, \ldots\}$ with $\leq$ | Power set $P(\{a, b\}) = \{\emptyset, \{a\}, \{b\}, \{a, b\}\}$ with $\subseteq$ | In the power set example, $\{a\}$ and $\{b\}$ are **incomparable** (neither $\{a\} \subseteq \{b\}$ nor $\{b\} \subseteq \{a\}$), making it only partially ordered, not well-ordered. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks** with a typical split of **7 marks for part (a)** and **7 marks for part (b)**. Students must answer **one full question** out of the two alternatives provided. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]** **(a)** Prove that the Well Ordering Principle implies the Principle of Mathematical Induction. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 (1 mark) — State both principles clearly:** - *WOP:* $\forall S \subseteq \mathbb{N}, S \neq \emptyset \Rightarrow \exists m \in S: m \leq s \; \forall s \in S$. - *Induction:* $P(1) \land \forall n \in \mathbb{N}, [P(n) \Rightarrow P(n+1)] \Rightarrow \forall n \in \mathbb{N}, P(n)$. **Step 2 (1 mark) — Assume for contradiction:** Suppose $P(1)$ is true and $P(n) \Rightarrow P(n+1)$ holds for all $n$, but $P(n)$ fails for some $n$. **Step 3 (1 mark) — Define counterexample set:** $$S = \{n \in \mathbb{N} \mid P(n) \text{ is false}\}$$ By assumption, $S \neq \emptyset$. **Step 4 (2 marks) — Apply WOP:** Since $S \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ and $S \neq \emptyset$, by WOP, $\exists m = \min(S)$. **Step 5 (1 mark) — Show $m \neq 1$:** Since $P(1)$ is true, $1 \notin S$, so $m \geq 2$. **Step 6 (1 mark) — Derive contradiction:** Since $m \geq 2$, $m-1 \in \mathbb{N}$ and $m-1 \notin S$ (because $m$ is the least element of $S$). So $P(m-1)$ is true. By the inductive hypothesis, $P(m-1) \Rightarrow P(m)$, so $P(m)$ is true. But $m \in S$ means $P(m)$ is false — contradiction. **Step 7 (1 mark) — Conclude:** $S = \emptyset$, so $P(n)$ holds for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$. $\blacksquare$ --- **(b)** Using the Well Ordering Principle, prove that for every integer $n \geq 1$, the sum of the first $n$ positive integers is $\frac{n(n+1)}{2}$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 (1 mark) — State what to prove:** $$P(n): \quad 1 + 2 + 3 + \cdots + n = \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \quad \forall n \in \mathbb{N}$$ **Step 2 (1 mark) — Define the counterexample set:** $$S = \{n \in \mathbb{N} \mid P(n) \text{ is false}\}$$ Assume $S \neq \emptyset$. **Step 3 (1 mark) — Apply WOP:** $S$ has a least element $m = \min(S)$. **Step 4 (1 mark) — Base case check:** For $m = 1$: $1 = \frac{1 \cdot 2}{2} = 1$ ✓. So $1 \notin S$, meaning $m \geq 2$. **Step 5 (1 mark) — Inductive equality:** Since $m-1 \notin S$ and $m-1 \in \mathbb{N}$, $P(m-1)$ holds: $$1 + 2 + \cdots + (m-1) = \frac{(m-1)m}{2}$$ **Step 6 (1 mark) — Add $m$ to both sides:** $$1 + 2 + \cdots + (m-1) + m = \frac{(m-1)m}{2} + m = \frac{m(m-1) + 2m}{2} = \frac{m^2 + m}{2} = \frac{m(m+1)}{2}$$ **Step 7 (1 mark) — Contradiction:** So $P(m)$ is true, but $m \in S$ means $P(m)$ is false — contradiction. Hence $S = \emptyset$. $\blacksquare$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 1]** **(a)** State the Well Ordering Principle. Prove that the Principle of Mathematical Induction implies the Well Ordering Principle. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 (1 mark) — State WOP:** Every non-empty subset of $\mathbb{N}$ has a least element. **Step 2 (1 mark) — Assume contrary:** Let $S \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ with $S \neq \emptyset$. Suppose $S$ has no least element. **Step 3 (1 mark) — Construct predicate:** Define $$P(n) = \text{``} \forall k \in \mathbb{N}, 1 \leq k \leq n \Rightarrow k \notin S \text{''}$$ **Step 4 (1 mark) — Base case:** $P(1)$: If $1 \in S$, then $1 = \min(S)$, contradicting that $S$ has no least element. So $1 \notin S$, and $P(1)$ is true. **Step 5 (1 mark) — Inductive step:** Assume $P(n)$ is true (no integer from $1$ to $n$ is in $S$). If $(n+1) \in S$, then $(n+1)$ would be the smallest element of $S$ (since no smaller positive integer is in $S$), contradicting the assumption. So $(n+1) \notin S$, and $P(n+1)$ is true. **Step 6 (1 mark) — Apply induction:** By mathematical induction, $P(n)$ is true for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$, meaning $S = \emptyset$. **Step 7 (1 mark) — Contradiction:** This contradicts $S \neq \emptyset$. Therefore, $S$ must have a least element. $\blacksquare$ --- **(b)** Show that the set of all positive even integers is well-ordered. Is the set of all integers well-ordered? Justify. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 (1 mark) — Define the set:** Let $E = \{2, 4, 6, 8, \ldots\} = \{2k \mid k \in \mathbb{N}\}$. **Step 2 (2 marks) — Prove E is well-ordered:** Let $T \subseteq E$ with $T \neq \emptyset$. Then $T/2 = \{n/2 \mid n \in T\} \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ and $T/2 \neq \emptyset$. By WOP, $T/2$ has a least element $m = \min(T/2)$. Then $2m \in T$ and $2m = \min(T)$ (since $m$ is the smallest half). So $E$ is well-ordered. **Step 3 (1 mark) — Address integers:** The set $\mathbb{Z} = \{\ldots, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$ is not well-ordered. **Step 4 (2 marks) — Justification:** Consider $\mathbb{Z}$ itself — it is non-empty, but for any candidate minimum $m \in \mathbb{Z}$, the integer $m - 1$ is also in $\mathbb{Z}$ and $m - 1 < m$. Hence, no least element exists in $\mathbb{Z}$. **Step 5 (1 mark) — Conclusion:** $E$ is well-ordered with $\min(E) = 2$, but $\mathbb{Z}$ is **not** well-ordered because it is unbounded below. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse WOP with the Well-Foundedness property.** WOP requires a *total* order; well-foundedness is more general. > 2. **Never state WOP for $\mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{Q}$.** It is **exclusive to $\mathbb{N}$** (or any set isomorphic to $\mathbb{N}$). > 3. **In proof by WOP, always verify $S \neq \emptyset$ explicitly** before applying the principle. This is the most common deduction. > 4. **The least element is unique** — students often lose 1 mark by not proving uniqueness via antisymmetry. > 5. **In the induction equivalence proof, students forget to check $m \neq 1$** — losing 1 mark for incomplete case analysis. > 6. **Do not confuse $\min(S)$ with $\inf(S)$.** The minimum is an actual member of $S$; the infimum may not be in $S$ (e.g., $\inf\{1/n\} = 0 \notin S$). --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist:** - **Definition:** WOP states every non-empty $S \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ has a least element $m \in S$ with $m \leq s \; \forall s \in S$. - **Logical Equivalence:** WOP $\iff$ Mathematical Induction $\iff$ Strong Induction (all three are logically equivalent over $\mathbb{N}$). - **Domain Restriction:** WOP applies **ONLY** to $\mathbb{N}$ (positive integers) and its subsets. It **fails** for $\mathbb{Z}$, $\mathbb{Q}$, $\mathbb{R}$. - **Uniqueness:** The least element of a well-ordered set is **unique** (by antisymmetry of $\leq$). - **Proof Technique:** Proof by Well-Ordering = Proof by Minimal Counterexample. Steps: 1. Assume contrary (set of counterexamples is non-empty). 2. Apply WOP to get the least counterexample. 3. Show this leads to a contradiction. 4. Conclude the original set is empty. - **Security Applications:** - Termination proofs for the Euclidean Algorithm (used in RSA). - Soundness of cryptographic protocol verifications. - Proofs that every integer $n > 1$ has a unique prime factorization (**Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic**). - Bounds on brute-force attack complexity. - **Key Identities to Remember:** - Sum: $1 + 2 + \cdots + n = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}$ - Geometric: $1 + 2 + 4 + \cdots + 2^{n-1} = 2^n - 1$ - Divisibility: $a \mid b \iff \exists k \in \mathbb{N}: b = ak$ - **Common Mistake:** Confusing "well-ordered" with "ordered" — an ordered set need not have a least element (e.g., $\mathbb{Z}$ is ordered but not well-ordered). - **Pedagogical Tip:** When asked to "prove by WOP," always begin with *"Suppose, for contradiction, that the statement fails for some element of $\mathbb{N}$."* This phrasing is a **guaranteed KTU scoring trigger**. - **Historical Note:** The WOP is one of the **Peano Axioms (1889)** that rigorously define the natural numbers. It is named in honor of Giuseppe Peano's formalization work. - **Mark Distribution Pattern (KTU 2024):** - Part A (3 marks): Definition + 1 example + 1 counterexample. - Part B (14 marks): 7 marks for proof + 7 marks for application/computation. - Always include a diagram or counterexample table to score full marks on "explain" questions.

topics.title: Bezout's Identity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT413 - FOUNDATIONS OF SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bezout's Identity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bezout's Identity (Bezout's Lemma):** Let $a$ and $b$ be positive integers with $\gcd(a, b) = d$. Then there exist integers $x$ and $y$ (which may be negative, zero, or positive) such that:
> $$ax + by = d = \gcd(a, b)$$

In simpler terms, Bezout's Identity states that the **Greatest Common Divisor (GCD)** of two integers can always be expressed as a **linear combination** of those two integers. The integers $x$ and $y$ are called **Bezout Coefficients** (or Bezout's multipliers).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlights for KTU 2024:**
> - Bezout's Identity is foundational to the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**.
> - It is a critical prerequisite for computing **Modular Multiplicative Inverses**.
> - Directly applied in **RSA Cryptosystem** key generation and decryption.
> - Listed under Module 1: *Number Theory & Cryptographic Foundations*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a cashier and you have unlimited **₹7 coins** and **₹11 coins**. A customer walks in and asks: *"Can you pay me exactly ₹1 using only these two coin types (giving change if needed, i.e., negative number of coins allowed)?"*

Your job is to find integers $x$ and $y$ such that:

$$7x + 11y = 1$$

Since $\gcd(7, 11) = 1$, Bezout's Identity **guarantees** that such a solution exists. One valid solution is $x = 8$ and $y = -5$, because:

$$7(8) + 11(-5) = 56 - 55 = 1$$

This means you can give the customer **8 coins of ₹7** and take back **5 coins of ₹11** — a net payment of exactly ₹1. This is the *essence* of Bezout's Identity: **no matter what two numbers you choose, their GCD can always be "manufactured" by adding and subtracting multiples of them**.

### 1.3 Standard Mathematical Constants/Metrics

- **$\gcd(a, b)$** — Greatest Common Divisor, always a **positive integer** ≥ 1.
- **Bezout Coefficients** $x, y \in \mathbb{Z}$ (the set of all integers, including negatives).
- **Coefficient Range Bound:** $\vert x \vert \leq b/d$ and $\vert y \vert \leq a/d$, where $d = \gcd(a, b)$.
- **Existence Standard:** Bezout's Identity always produces coefficients, but they are **NOT unique** — there are infinitely many pairs $(x, y)$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The "Why" and "How"

**Why does Bezout's Identity hold?**
The identity is a direct consequence of the **Well-Ordering Principle** applied to the set of all positive integer linear combinations of $a$ and $b$. Consider the set:

$$S = \{ax + by \mid x, y \in \mathbb{Z},\ ax + by > 0\}$$

- This set is non-empty because $a, b > 0$ implies $a \cdot 1 + b \cdot 0 = a > 0 \in S$.
- By the Well-Ordering Principle, $S$ has a **smallest element** $d$.
- It can be proven (via the Euclidean division algorithm) that $d$ must divide both $a$ and $b$, hence $d = \gcd(a, b)$.
- Since $d \in S$, there exist integers $x, y$ such that $ax + by = d$. $\blacksquare$

**How is it used computationally?**
The **Extended Euclidean Algorithm (EEA)** is the algorithmic engine that finds $x$ and $y$ efficiently in $O(\log(\min(a, b)))$ time.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Extended Euclidean Algorithm

1. **Apply the standard Euclidean Algorithm** to compute $\gcd(a, b)$ using successive divisions:
   $$a = bq_1 + r_1$$
   $$b = r_1q_2 + r_2$$
   $$r_1 = r_2q_3 + r_3$$
   $$\vdots$$
   $$r_{n-2} = r_{n-1}q_n + r_n$$
   Until $r_n = 0$, at which point $r_{n-1} = \gcd(a, b)$.

2. **Back-substitute** from the second-to-last equation upward, expressing each remainder as a linear combination of $a$ and $b$.

3. **Terminate** when the linear combination equals $\gcd(a, b)$. The coefficients of $a$ and $b$ at this stage are the Bezout coefficients $x$ and $y$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Theorem** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Condition / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Bezout's Identity | $ax + by = \gcd(a, b)$ | $a, b \in \mathbb{Z}^+$, $x, y \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| Coprime Condition | $\gcd(a, b) = 1 \Rightarrow ax + by = 1$ | $a$ and $b$ are relatively prime |
| Modular Inverse via Bezout | $ax \equiv 1 \pmod{b}$ | Exists iff $\gcd(a, b) = 1$, then $x \equiv a^{-1} \pmod{b}$ |
| Coefficient Bound | $\vert x \vert \leq b/d$, $\vert y \vert \leq a/d$ | $d = \gcd(a, b)$ |
| General Bezout Form | $ax + by = kd$, for any $k \in \mathbb{Z}$ | All multiples of $d$ are representable |
| Euclidean Division | $a = bq + r$, where $0 \leq r < b$ | Foundation of EEA |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In **cryptography**, finding $x$ such that $ax \equiv 1 \pmod{b}$ (the modular inverse) is the computational core of **RSA decryption**, **Diffie-Hellman key exchange**, and **Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC)**. Bezout's Identity guarantees this inverse exists whenever $a$ and $b$ are coprime.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Finding Bezout Coefficients for $a = 252$, $b = 198$

**Step 1: Apply the standard Euclidean Algorithm.**

$$252 = 198 \cdot 1 + 54 \quad \text{(divide 252 by 198, remainder 54)}$$

$$198 = 54 \cdot 3 + 36 \quad \text{(divide 198 by 54, remainder 36)}$$

$$54 = 36 \cdot 1 + 18 \quad \text{(divide 54 by 36, remainder 18)}$$

$$36 = 18 \cdot 2 + 0 \quad \text{(divide 36 by 18, remainder 0 — STOP)}$$

**Step 2: Identify the GCD.** From the last non-zero remainder, $\gcd(252, 198) = 18$.

**Step 3: Back-substitute to express 18 as a linear combination of 252 and 198.**

Starting from the third equation: $54 = 36 \cdot 1 + 18$, rearrange to isolate 18:

$$18 = 54 - 36 \cdot 1$$

Substitute $36 = 198 - 54 \cdot 3$:

$$18 = 54 - (198 - 54 \cdot 3) \cdot 1$$

$$18 = 54 - 198 + 54 \cdot 3$$

$$18 = 54 \cdot 4 - 198 \cdot 1$$

Substitute $54 = 252 - 198 \cdot 1$:

$$18 = (252 - 198 \cdot 1) \cdot 4 - 198 \cdot 1$$

$$18 = 252 \cdot 4 - 198 \cdot 4 - 198 \cdot 1$$

$$18 = 252 \cdot 4 - 198 \cdot 5$$

**Step 4: Verify the result.**

$$252(4) + 198(-5) = 1008 - 990 = 18 \checkmark$$

Therefore, the Bezout coefficients are $x = 4$ and $y = -5$, confirming:

$$252(4) + 198(-5) = 18 = \gcd(252, 198)$$

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Finding the Modular Inverse of $17 \pmod{43}$

We need to find $x$ such that $17x \equiv 1 \pmod{43}$.

**Step 1: Apply the Euclidean Algorithm to find $\gcd(17, 43)$.**

$$43 = 17 \cdot 2 + 9$$

$$17 = 9 \cdot 1 + 8$$

$$9 = 8 \cdot 1 + 1$$

$$8 = 1 \cdot 8 + 0$$

So $\gcd(17, 43) = 1$. Since the GCD is 1, a modular inverse exists.

**Step 2: Back-substitute to express 1 as a linear combination.**

From $9 = 8 \cdot 1 + 1$:

$$1 = 9 - 8 \cdot 1$$

Substitute $8 = 17 - 9 \cdot 1$:

$$1 = 9 - (17 - 9) \cdot 1 = 9 \cdot 2 - 17 \cdot 1$$

Substitute $9 = 43 - 17 \cdot 2$:

$$1 = (43 - 17 \cdot 2) \cdot 2 - 17 = 43 \cdot 2 - 17 \cdot 4 - 17 = 43 \cdot 2 - 17 \cdot 5$$

Therefore:

$$17 \cdot (-5) + 43 \cdot 2 = 1$$

**Step 3: Extract the modular inverse.**

$$17 \cdot (-5) \equiv 1 \pmod{43}$$

So $x = -5 \pmod{43}$, which means $x = -5 + 43 = 38$.

**Step 4: Verify the inverse.**

$$17 \cdot 38 = 646 = 43 \cdot 15 + 1 = 645 + 1 \checkmark$$

Thus, $17^{-1} \equiv 38 \pmod{43}$.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: General Solution Structure

If $(x_0, y_0)$ is one particular solution to $ax + by = d$, then the **general solution** is:

$$x = x_0 + \frac{b}{d} \cdot t, \quad y = y_0 - \frac{a}{d} \cdot t, \quad \text{for any } t \in \mathbb{Z}$$

This explains why infinitely many coefficient pairs exist — they form an arithmetic progression indexed by $t$.

| **Property** | **Statement** | **Example ($a=6, b=4, d=2$)** |
|---|---|---|
| Particular Solution | $6(1) + 4(-1) = 2$ | $x_0 = 1, y_0 = -1$ |
| General Solution | $x = 1 + 2t$, $y = -1 - 3t$ | $t = 0: (1,-1)$; $t=1: (3,-4)$ |
| Modular Inverse Form | $x \equiv x_0 \pmod{b/d}$ | $x \equiv 1 \pmod 2$ |
| Existence Test | $d \mid c$ is required for $ax+by=c$ to have solutions | $c=3$: NO solution for $6x+4y=3$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flowchart: Extended Euclidean Algorithm for Bezout Coefficients

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input a, b] --> B[Apply Euclidean Algorithm]
    B --> C["a = b*q1 + r1"]
    C --> D["b = r1*q2 + r2"]
    D --> E["r1 = r2*q3 + r3"]
    E --> F{ri = 0?}
    F -- No --> G[Continue division]
    G --> E
    F -- Yes --> H["gcd = r(n-1)"]
    H --> I[Back-Substitute Remainders]
    I --> J[Express gcd as a*x + b*y]
    J --> K{Goal: Modular Inverse?}
    K -- Yes --> L["x mod b is the inverse of a"]
    K -- No --> M[Return Bezout Coefficients x, y]
    L --> N[End]
    M --> N
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture: Bezout's Identity in RSA Cryptosystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input["Input Parameters"]
        A1[Public Exponent e]
        A2[Modulus phi n]
    end
    
    subgraph EEA["Extended Euclidean Algorithm"]
        B1[Apply EEA to e and phi n]
        B2[Find x, y such that e*x + phi n*y = 1]
        B3[Reduce x modulo phi n]
    end
    
    subgraph Output["Output"]
        C1[Private Key d = x mod phi n]
        C2[Verification: e*d mod phi n = 1]
    end
    
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Map: Bezout's Identity in Number Theory

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[GCD Definition] --> Q[Euclidean Algorithm]
    Q --> R[Bezout's Identity]
    R --> S[Extended Euclidean Algorithm]
    S --> T[Modular Inverse Computation]
    T --> U[RSA Decryption]
    T --> V[Diffie-Hellman Exchange]
    T --> W[ECC Point Operations]
    R --> X[Linear Diophantine Equations]
    X --> Y[Cryptographic Protocol Design]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** State Bezout's Identity. Under what condition does it guarantee the existence of a modular multiplicative inverse of $a$ modulo $b$?

**Model Answer:**
Bezout's Identity states that for any two positive integers $a$ and $b$, there exist integers $x$ and $y$ such that:

$$ax + by = \gcd(a, b)$$

The modular multiplicative inverse of $a$ modulo $b$ exists **if and only if** $\gcd(a, b) = 1$ (i.e., $a$ and $b$ are coprime). In that case, $ax \equiv 1 \pmod{b}$, meaning $x$ is the inverse of $a$ modulo $b$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** If $\gcd(a, b) = 1$ and $ax + by = 1$, what can you conclude about $x$ and $y$? Mention one practical application.

**Model Answer:**
If $\gcd(a, b) = 1$ and $ax + by = 1$, then $x$ is the **modular multiplicative inverse** of $a$ modulo $b$ (and equivalently, $y$ is the inverse of $b$ modulo $a$). **Application:** This identity is used to compute the **private key $d$** in the RSA cryptosystem, where $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a)** Find integers $x$ and $y$ such that $161x + 28y = \gcd(161, 28)$ using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the Euclidean Algorithm.

$$161 = 28 \cdot 5 + 21$$

$$28 = 21 \cdot 1 + 7$$

$$21 = 7 \cdot 3 + 0$$

So $\gcd(161, 28) = 7$.

**Step 2:** Back-substitute from the second equation.

$$7 = 28 - 21 \cdot 1$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $21 = 161 - 28 \cdot 5$:

$$7 = 28 - (161 - 28 \cdot 5)$$

$$7 = 28 - 161 + 28 \cdot 5 = 28 \cdot 6 - 161$$

So $\boxed{x = -1, y = 6}$ and $161(-1) + 28(6) = -161 + 168 = 7$. $\checkmark$

---

**(b)** Using Bezout's Identity, find the modular inverse of $23 \pmod{100}$, or prove that no such inverse exists. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute $\gcd(23, 100)$ using the Euclidean Algorithm.

$$100 = 23 \cdot 4 + 8$$

$$23 = 8 \cdot 2 + 7$$

$$8 = 7 \cdot 1 + 1$$

$$7 = 1 \cdot 7 + 0$$

So $\gcd(23, 100) = 1$. The inverse exists.

**Step 2:** Back-substitute.

$$1 = 8 - 7 \cdot 1$$

Substitute $7 = 23 - 8 \cdot 2$:

$$1 = 8 - (23 - 8 \cdot 2) = 8 \cdot 3 - 23$$

Substitute $8 = 100 - 23 \cdot 4$:

$$1 = (100 - 23 \cdot 4) \cdot 3 - 23 = 100 \cdot 3 - 23 \cdot 12 - 23 = 100 \cdot 3 - 23 \cdot 13$$

So $23 \cdot (-13) \equiv 1 \pmod{100}$, which gives $x = -13 \equiv 87 \pmod{100}$.

**Step 3:** Verification: $23 \times 87 = 2001 = 100 \times 20 + 1$. $\checkmark$

Therefore, $23^{-1} \equiv 87 \pmod{100}$.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Solve the linear Diophantine equation $15x + 35y = 100$ for integer solutions $(x, y)$. If no solution exists, justify using Bezout's Identity. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute $\gcd(15, 35)$.

$$35 = 15 \cdot 2 + 5$$

$$15 = 5 \cdot 3 + 0$$

So $\gcd(15, 35) = 5$.

**Step 2:** Check the divisibility condition. For the equation $15x + 35y = 100$ to have integer solutions, we need $\gcd(15, 35) \mid 100$. Since $5 \mid 100$ (as $100 = 5 \cdot 20$), solutions **do exist**.

**Step 3:** Divide the entire equation by 5:

$$3x + 7y = 20$$

**Step 4:** Apply Bezout's Identity to express $5 = 15(-1) + 35(1)$, then scale:

$$100 = 15(-20) + 35(20)$$

So one particular solution is $x_0 = -20, y_0 = 20$.

**Step 5:** General solution form:

$$x = -20 + 7t, \quad y = 20 - 3t, \quad t \in \mathbb{Z}$$

**Step 6:** Sample integer solutions: $t = 3 \Rightarrow (1, 11)$; $t = 2 \Rightarrow (-6, 14)$; etc.

---

**(b)** In the RSA cryptosystem, the public exponent $e = 17$ and $\phi(n) = 3120$. Using Bezout's Identity, compute the private key $d$ such that $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Verify $\gcd(17, 3120) = 1$ using the Euclidean Algorithm.

$$3120 = 17 \cdot 183 + 9$$

$$17 = 9 \cdot 1 + 8$$

$$9 = 8 \cdot 1 + 1$$

$$8 = 1 \cdot 8 + 0$$

So $\gcd(17, 3120) = 1$. The inverse exists.

**Step 2:** Back-substitute to find Bezout coefficients.

$$1 = 9 - 8 \cdot 1$$

$$1 = 9 - (17 - 9) = 9 \cdot 2 - 17 \cdot 1$$

$$1 = (3120 - 17 \cdot 183) \cdot 2 - 17 = 3120 \cdot 2 - 17 \cdot 366 - 17 = 3120 \cdot 2 - 17 \cdot 367$$

So $17 \cdot (-367) \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$.

**Step 3:** Reduce modulo 3120.

$$d = -367 \pmod{3120} = 3120 - 367 = 2753$$

**Step 4:** Verification: $17 \times 2753 = 46801 = 3120 \times 15 + 1$. $\checkmark$

Therefore, the private key is $d = 2753$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Errors in Back-Substitution:** The most common mistake is mishandling negative signs while substituting remainders. Always re-substitute the *original* remainder expression, not a partially simplified version.
> 2. **Forgetting the Modulo Reduction:** Students often leave $d$ as a negative number (e.g., $d = -367$) instead of converting it to its positive residue modulo $\phi(n)$. This will cause RSA decryption to fail entirely.
> 3. **Skipping the GCD Check:** Failing to verify $\gcd(a, b) = 1$ before assuming a modular inverse exists costs 2–3 marks outright. Always begin with the Euclidean Algorithm.
> 4. **Off-by-One in the Final Remainder:** Students sometimes incorrectly identify the GCD as the last remainder before zero — make sure you stop at $r_{n-1}$ (the **last non-zero** remainder), not $r_n$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Bezout's Identity** states: $\forall a, b \in \mathbb{Z}^+$, $\exists x, y \in \mathbb{Z}$ such that $ax + by = \gcd(a, b)$.
- **Modular inverse** of $a \pmod b$ exists **iff** $\gcd(a, b) = 1$.
- The **Extended Euclidean Algorithm (EEA)** is the constructive proof of Bezout's Identity — it always finds valid $x, y$.
- **Solution non-uniqueness:** All solutions form a parametric family $x = x_0 + (b/d)t$, $y = y_0 - (a/d)t$, $t \in \mathbb{Z}$.
- **Linear Diophantine equation** $ax + by = c$ has solutions **iff** $\gcd(a, b) \mid c$.
- **Cryptographic applications:** RSA private key generation, Diffie-Hellman parameter setup, and ECC scalar inversion all rely on Bezout's Identity.
- **Coefficient bounds:** $\vert x \vert \leq b/d$ and $\vert y \vert \leq a/d$ — useful for verifying computed coefficients.
- **Verification step is mandatory** in KTU exams — always plug your $x, y$ back into the original equation to confirm $\gcd(a, b)$.
- **Time complexity** of EEA is $O(\log(\min(a, b)))$ — efficient even for cryptographic-sized integers (e.g., 2048-bit RSA keys).
- **Generalization:** Bezout's Identity extends to more than two integers: $\gcd(a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n)$ can be written as a linear combination of all of them.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modular Arithmetic - Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT413 - FOUNDATIONS OF SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Modular Arithmetic - Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Modular Arithmetic** is a system of arithmetic for integers, where numbers "wrap around" upon reaching a certain value called the **modulus**. Two integers $a$ and $b$ are said to be **congruent modulo $n$** (written as $a \equiv b \pmod{n}$) if and only if their difference $(a - b)$ is an integer multiple of $n$. Formally:

$$a \equiv b \pmod{n} \iff n \mid (a - b)$$

where $n > 0$ is the **modulus** and the symbol $\mid$ denotes "divides exactly".

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The modulus $n$ in congruence relations is conventionally a **positive integer ($n \geq 2$)** in cryptographic and security applications. The set of all possible residues modulo $n$ is called the **Residue Class System** $\mathbb{Z}_n = \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, n-1\}$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The 12-Hour Clock

Imagine the hour hand on a standard **12-hour analog clock**. If the current time is **10:00 AM** and we add **5 hours**, the clock does not read 15:00 (which would be a 24-hour representation). Instead, the hour hand wraps around and shows **3:00 PM**.

Mathematically, in standard arithmetic: $10 + 5 = 15$.
But in modular arithmetic with modulus 12: $10 + 5 \equiv 3 \pmod{12}$.

Similarly:
- $10 + 5 = 15 \equiv 3 \pmod{12}$ (because $15 - 12 = 3$)
- $10 + 15 = 25 \equiv 1 \pmod{12}$ (because $25 - 2(12) = 1$)
- $10 - 5 = 5 \equiv 5 \pmod{12}$ (trivial, since result is already reduced)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Modular arithmetic is simply regular integer arithmetic where the number line is bent into a **circle of circumference $n$**. Every calculation is performed on this finite cyclic number line, ensuring results always lie in the set $\{0, 1, \ldots, n-1\}$.

### 1.3 The Modulo Operation

The **modulo operation** (denoted $\bmod$) returns the remainder after integer division. For any integer $a$ and positive integer $n$, there exist unique integers $q$ (quotient) and $r$ (remainder) such that:

$$a = n \cdot q + r, \quad \text{where } 0 \leq r < n$$

The value $r$ is denoted as $a \bmod n$. The congruence $a \equiv b \pmod{n}$ is equivalent to saying $(a \bmod n) = (b \bmod n)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Context:** Modular arithmetic is the **mathematical backbone of modern cryptography**. Algorithms such as **RSA, Diffie-Hellman, and Elliptic Curve Cryptography** rely entirely on modular exponentiation, modular inverses, and the structure of $\mathbb{Z}_n$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Properties of Modular Arithmetic

For all integers $a$, $b$, $c$, and a positive modulus $n$, the following properties hold:

#### 2.1.1 Reflexive Property
$$a \equiv a \pmod{n}$$
Every integer is congruent to itself.

#### 2.1.2 Symmetric Property
$$\text{If } a \equiv b \pmod{n}, \text{ then } b \equiv a \pmod{n}$$

#### 2.1.3 Transitive Property
$$\text{If } a \equiv b \pmod{n} \text{ and } b \equiv c \pmod{n}, \text{ then } a \equiv c \pmod{n}$$

These three properties together establish that congruence modulo $n$ is an **equivalence relation** on the set of integers $\mathbb{Z}$.

#### 2.1.4 Compatibility with Addition
$$\text{If } a \equiv b \pmod{n} \text{ and } c \equiv d \pmod{n}, \text{ then } (a + c) \equiv (b + d) \pmod{n}$$

#### 2.1.5 Compatibility with Subtraction
$$\text{If } a \equiv b \pmod{n} \text{ and } c \equiv d \pmod{n}, \text{ then } (a - c) \equiv (b - d) \pmod{n}$$

#### 2.1.6 Compatibility with Multiplication
$$\text{If } a \equiv b \pmod{n} \text{ and } c \equiv d \pmod{n}, \text{ then } (a \cdot c) \equiv (b \cdot d) \pmod{n}$$

#### 2.1.7 Multiplication by a Constant
$$\text{If } a \equiv b \pmod{n}, \text{ then } (k \cdot a) \equiv (k \cdot b) \pmod{n} \quad \text{for any integer } k$$

#### 2.1.8 Power Compatibility
$$\text{If } a \equiv b \pmod{n}, \text{ then } a^k \equiv b^k \pmod{n} \quad \text{for any non-negative integer } k$$

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Property Name** | **Mathematical Statement** | **Engineering Utility** |
|---|---|---|
| Reflexive | $a \equiv a \pmod{n}$ | Foundational identity property |
| Symmetric | $a \equiv b \Rightarrow b \equiv a$ | Reversibility of congruence |
| Transitive | $a \equiv b, b \equiv c \Rightarrow a \equiv c$ | Chain reduction in proofs |
| Addition | $(a+c) \equiv (b+d) \pmod{n}$ | Sum preservation in ciphertext |
| Subtraction | $(a-c) \equiv (b-d) \pmod{n}$ | Difference preservation |
| Multiplication | $(a \cdot c) \equiv (b \cdot d) \pmod{n}$ | Product preservation in modular exponentiation |
| Scalar Multiplication | $ka \equiv kb \pmod{n}$ | Coefficient reduction |
| Power Rule | $a^k \equiv b^k \pmod{n}$ | Core of RSA encryption/decryption |
| Euclidean Reduction | $a \equiv a \bmod n$ | Computational simplification |
| Modular Inverse | $a \cdot a^{-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ | RSA private key derivation |
| Cancellation Law | If $\gcd(k,n)=1$, then $ka \equiv kb \Rightarrow a \equiv b$ | Simplifying equations |
| Distributive Law | $(a + b) \cdot c \equiv (a \cdot c) + (b \cdot c) \pmod{n}$ | Algebraic expansion in $\mathbb{Z}_n$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule — The Cancellation Law:** Cancellation in modular arithmetic is **NOT** always valid. We can cancel $k$ from both sides of $ka \equiv kb \pmod{n}$ **only if** $\gcd(k, n) = 1$ (i.e., $k$ and $n$ are **coprime**). This is a frequent KTU exam pitfall.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Field** | **Application of Modular Arithmetic** |
|---|---|
| **Public-Key Cryptography** | RSA encryption, Diffie-Hellman key exchange |
| **Hash Functions** | SHA family (modular addition of words) |
| **Error Detection** | Checksums, CRC codes, ISBN validation |
| **Computer Graphics** | Texture wrapping, shader coordinate tiling |
| **Database Systems** | Hash table indexing, modular hashing |
| **Networking** | IP address subnet masking |
| **Music Theory** | 12-tone equal temperament pitch classes |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Proof of the Compatibility with Multiplication

**Statement:** If $a \equiv b \pmod{n}$ and $c \equiv d \pmod{n}$, then $(a \cdot c) \equiv (b \cdot d) \pmod{n}$.

**Step-by-Step Proof:**

Let us begin with the given hypothesis. Since $a \equiv b \pmod{n}$, by definition of congruence there exists an integer $k_1$ such that:

$$a = b + k_1 \cdot n$$

Similarly, since $c \equiv d \pmod{n}$, there exists an integer $k_2$ such that:

$$c = d + k_2 \cdot n$$

Now compute the product $a \cdot c$ by substituting the expressions above:

$$a \cdot c = (b + k_1 \cdot n)(d + k_2 \cdot n)$$

Expand the right-hand side using the standard distributive property of integer multiplication:

$$a \cdot c = b \cdot d + b \cdot k_2 \cdot n + k_1 \cdot n \cdot d + k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot n^2$$

Factor out $n$ from the last three terms to expose the common structure:

$$a \cdot c = b \cdot d + n \cdot (b \cdot k_2 + k_1 \cdot d + k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot n)$$

Recognize that the bracketed expression is itself an integer. Let us denote it as $K = (b \cdot k_2 + k_1 \cdot d + k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot n)$, which is a valid integer because it is a sum of products of integers.

Thus we obtain:

$$a \cdot c = b \cdot d + K \cdot n$$

By the formal definition of congruence, this equation directly implies that $n$ divides the difference $(a \cdot c - b \cdot d)$, and therefore:

$$a \cdot c \equiv b \cdot d \pmod{n}$$

This completes the rigorous proof. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Proof of the Power Compatibility Rule

**Statement:** If $a \equiv b \pmod{n}$, then $a^k \equiv b^k \pmod{n}$ for any non-negative integer $k$.

**Proof by Mathematical Induction:**

**Base Case ($k = 1$):** Trivially, $a^1 = a$ and $b^1 = b$, so the statement $a \equiv b \pmod{n}$ holds by the given hypothesis.

**Inductive Hypothesis:** Assume that for some positive integer $k$, the statement $a^k \equiv b^k \pmod{n}$ holds true.

**Inductive Step:** We must show that $a^{k+1} \equiv b^{k+1} \pmod{n}$. Begin by writing:

$$a^{k+1} = a^k \cdot a$$

By the inductive hypothesis, $a^k \equiv b^k \pmod{n}$. By the given hypothesis, $a \equiv b \pmod{n}$. Now apply the **Multiplication Compatibility Property** (proven in Section 3.1) to these two congruences:

$$a^k \cdot a \equiv b^k \cdot b \pmod{n}$$

Simplify both sides:

$$a^{k+1} \equiv b^{k+1} \pmod{n}$$

This is exactly what we needed to show. By the principle of mathematical induction, the property holds for all non-negative integers $k$. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Worked Example: Evaluating $17^3 \pmod{5}$

We wish to compute $17^3 \bmod 5$ efficiently using the power compatibility property.

**Step 1:** Reduce the base 17 modulo 5:

$$17 = 3 \cdot 5 + 2 \implies 17 \equiv 2 \pmod{5}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the power compatibility rule to replace the base:

$$17^3 \equiv 2^3 \pmod{5}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the small exponent:

$$2^3 = 8$$

**Step 4:** Reduce the result modulo 5:

$$8 = 1 \cdot 5 + 3 \implies 8 \equiv 3 \pmod{5}$$

**Final Answer:** $17^3 \equiv 3 \pmod{5}$.

**Verification by direct computation:** $17^3 = 4913$. Then $4913 \div 5 = 982$ remainder $3$. Indeed, $4913 \equiv 3 \pmod{5}$. The result is consistent.

### 3.4 Worked Example: Modular Inverse via Extended Euclidean Algorithm

Find $x$ such that $7 \cdot x \equiv 1 \pmod{26}$.

**Step 1:** Verify that $\gcd(7, 26) = 1$ using the Euclidean Algorithm:

$$26 = 3 \cdot 7 + 5$$
$$7 = 1 \cdot 5 + 2$$
$$5 = 2 \cdot 2 + 1$$
$$2 = 2 \cdot 1 + 0$$

Since the last non-zero remainder is $1$, we have $\gcd(7, 26) = 1$, confirming that the inverse exists.

**Step 2:** Back-substitute to express 1 as a linear combination of 7 and 26:

From $5 = 26 - 3 \cdot 7$:

$$1 = 5 - 2 \cdot 2$$

Substitute $2 = 7 - 1 \cdot 5$:

$$1 = 5 - 2 \cdot (7 - 5) = 3 \cdot 5 - 2 \cdot 7$$

Substitute $5 = 26 - 3 \cdot 7$:

$$1 = 3 \cdot (26 - 3 \cdot 7) - 2 \cdot 7 = 3 \cdot 26 - 11 \cdot 7$$

**Step 3:** Read off the modular inverse:

$$-11 \cdot 7 \equiv 1 \pmod{26}$$

Reduce $-11$ to a positive residue modulo 26: $-11 + 26 = 15$.

**Final Answer:** $7^{-1} \equiv 15 \pmod{26}$.

**Verification:** $7 \cdot 15 = 105 = 4 \cdot 26 + 1 \equiv 1 \pmod{26}$. ✓

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Architecture of Modular Arithmetic

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the structural relationships between the core components of modular arithmetic and their applications in security:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Integer Set Z] --> B[Choose Modulus n]
    B --> C[Residue Class System Zn]
    C --> D1[Residue 0]
    C --> D2[Residue 1]
    C --> D3[Residue n-1]
    C --> E[Congruence Relation]
    E --> F1[Reflexive Property]
    E --> F2[Symmetric Property]
    E --> F3[Transitive Property]
    E --> G1[Addition Compatibility]
    E --> G2[Multiplication Compatibility]
    E --> G3[Power Compatibility]
    G2 --> H[Modular Inverse]
    H --> I{Inverse Exists?}
    I -- gcd a n = 1 --> J[Unique Inverse in Zn]
    I -- gcd a n greater than 1 --> K[Inverse Does Not Exist]
    G3 --> L[RSA Encryption]
    G3 --> M[Diffie Hellman Key Exchange]
    G1 --> N[Modular Exponentiation]
    N --> L
    N --> M
    L --> O[Cryptographic Security]
    M --> O
    H --> O
    J --> O
```

### 4.2 Modular Arithmetic Computation Flow

This diagram shows the algorithmic flow when computing a value modulo $n$:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Input Integer a] --> P2[a equals n times q plus r]
    P2 --> P3[Compute Quotient q equals floor of a divided by n]
    P2 --> P4[Compute Remainder r equals a minus n times q]
    P3 --> P5[Verify 0 less than or equal to r less than n]
    P4 --> P5
    P5 -- Valid --> P6[Output r equals a mod n]
    P5 -- Invalid --> P7[Adjust r and Recompute]
    P7 --> P6
    P6 --> P8[Apply to Cryptographic Operation]
    P8 --> P9[Encryption or Decryption or Hashing]
```

### 4.3 Hierarchy of Modular Properties

```mermaid
graph TD
    R0[Modular Arithmetic Zn] --> R1[Equivalence Relation]
    R1 --> R1a[Reflexive]
    R1 --> R1b[Symmetric]
    R1 --> R1c[Transitive]
    R0 --> R2[Algebraic Compatibility]
    R2 --> R2a[Addition Closure]
    R2 --> R2b[Multiplication Closure]
    R2 --> R2c[Distributive Law]
    R0 --> R3[Advanced Operations]
    R3 --> R3a[Modular Exponentiation]
    R3 --> R3b[Modular Inverse]
    R3 --> R3c[Power Compatibility]
    R0 --> R4[Number Theoretic Tools]
    R4 --> R4a[Euclidean Algorithm]
    R4 --> R4b[Euler Totient Function]
    R4 --> R4c[Fermat Little Theorem]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**State and explain the reflexive, symmetric, and transitive properties of modular arithmetic with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The properties of modular arithmetic establish that congruence modulo $n$ is an equivalence relation. The three properties are:

1. **Reflexive Property:** For any integer $a$, $a \equiv a \pmod{n}$. Example: $7 \equiv 7 \pmod{11}$ trivially.

2. **Symmetric Property:** If $a \equiv b \pmod{n}$, then $b \equiv a \pmod{n}$. Example: Since $17 \equiv 5 \pmod{12}$, it follows that $5 \equiv 17 \pmod{12}$.

3. **Transitive Property:** If $a \equiv b \pmod{n}$ and $b \equiv c \pmod{n}$, then $a \equiv c \pmod{n}$. Example: $19 \equiv 7 \pmod{12}$ and $7 \equiv 1 \pmod{6}$ does not chain, but $19 \equiv 7 \pmod{12}$ and $7 \equiv 1 \pmod{6}$ implies $19 \equiv 1 \pmod{6}$ (transitivity must be over the same modulus).

*(Examiner's Note: Students often forget that transitivity requires the same modulus $n$ for both congruences.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define the modular inverse of an integer $a$ modulo $n$. Under what condition does a modular inverse exist?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **modular inverse** of an integer $a$ modulo $n$ is an integer $x$ such that:

$$a \cdot x \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$$

The inverse is denoted $a^{-1} \pmod{n}$.

**Existence Condition:** A modular inverse of $a$ modulo $n$ exists **if and only if** $\gcd(a, n) = 1$, i.e., $a$ and $n$ are **coprime** (relatively prime). This is a direct consequence of **Bézout's Identity**: there exist integers $x$ and $y$ such that $a \cdot x + n \cdot y = \gcd(a, n) = 1$, which reduces to $a \cdot x \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$.

**Example:** The inverse of $3$ modulo $7$ is $5$ because $3 \cdot 5 = 15 \equiv 1 \pmod{7}$. Here $\gcd(3, 7) = 1$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define modular arithmetic. Prove the multiplication compatibility property of modular arithmetic. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 Marks):** Modular arithmetic is a system of arithmetic where integers reduce to a residue within the set $\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, n-1\}$. Two integers $a$ and $b$ are said to be congruent modulo $n$ (written $a \equiv b \pmod{n}$) if $n$ divides $(a - b)$ exactly.

**Proof (5 Marks):**

Given: $a \equiv b \pmod{n}$ and $c \equiv d \pmod{n}$.

By definition, there exist integers $k_1$ and $k_2$ such that $a = b + k_1 n$ and $c = d + k_2 n$.

Multiplying the two equations:

$$a \cdot c = (b + k_1 n)(d + k_2 n)$$

Expanding:

$$a \cdot c = bd + b k_2 n + k_1 n d + k_1 k_2 n^2$$

Factoring $n$ from the last three terms:

$$a \cdot c = bd + n(bk_2 + k_1 d + k_1 k_2 n)$$

Since $(bk_2 + k_1 d + k_1 k_2 n)$ is an integer, say $K$:

$$a \cdot c = bd + K \cdot n$$

Therefore $n$ divides $(ac - bd)$, which means $ac \equiv bd \pmod{n}$. $\blacksquare$

---

**(b)** Using modular arithmetic properties, compute the value of $3^{47} \bmod 7$ showing every step clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Find the multiplicative order. Compute successive powers of $3$ modulo $7$:

$$3^1 \equiv 3 \pmod{7}$$
$$3^2 = 9 \equiv 2 \pmod{7}$$
$$3^3 = 3 \cdot 2 = 6 \equiv -1 \pmod{7}$$
$$3^4 = 3 \cdot (-1) = -3 \equiv 4 \pmod{7}$$
$$3^5 = 3 \cdot 4 = 12 \equiv 5 \pmod{7}$$
$$3^6 = 3 \cdot 5 = 15 \equiv 1 \pmod{7}$$

The order of $3$ modulo $7$ is $6$ (this is consistent with Fermat's Little Theorem: $3^6 \equiv 1 \pmod{7}$ since $7$ is prime and $\gcd(3,7) = 1$).

**Step 2:** Reduce the exponent $47$ modulo $6$:

$$47 = 7 \cdot 6 + 5 \implies 47 \equiv 5 \pmod{6}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the exponent reduction:

$$3^{47} = 3^{6 \cdot 7 + 5} = (3^6)^7 \cdot 3^5 \equiv 1^7 \cdot 3^5 \equiv 3^5 \pmod{7}$$

**Step 4:** From Step 1, $3^5 \equiv 5 \pmod{7}$.

**Final Answer:** $3^{47} \equiv 5 \pmod{7}$.

**Verification:** $3^5 = 243$. $243 \div 7 = 34$ remainder $5$. ✓

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** State the properties of modular arithmetic related to addition, multiplication, and power. Show that modular arithmetic forms a commutative ring with identity under addition and multiplication modulo $n$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Properties (3 Marks):**
- **Addition Compatibility:** $a \equiv b, c \equiv d \pmod{n} \Rightarrow a+c \equiv b+d \pmod{n}$
- **Multiplication Compatibility:** $a \equiv b, c \equiv d \pmod{n} \Rightarrow ac \equiv bd \pmod{n}$
- **Power Compatibility:** $a \equiv b \pmod{n} \Rightarrow a^k \equiv b^k \pmod{n}$ for $k \geq 0$
- **Distributive Law:** $a(b+c) \equiv ab + ac \pmod{n}$

**Commutative Ring Proof (4 Marks):**

The set $\mathbb{Z}_n = \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, n-1\}$ with operations $+_n$ and $\cdot_n$ (addition and multiplication modulo $n$) forms a **commutative ring with identity** because:

1. **Closure:** For all $a, b \in \mathbb{Z}_n$, $(a +_n b) \in \mathbb{Z}_n$ and $(a \cdot_n b) \in \mathbb{Z}_n$ (since $0 \leq$ result $< n$).

2. **Associativity:** $a +_n (b +_n c) = (a +_n b) +_n c$ and $a \cdot_n (b \cdot_n c) = (a \cdot_n b) \cdot_n c$. (Inherited from integer arithmetic.)

3. **Commutativity:** $a +_n b = b +_n a$ and $a \cdot_n b = b \cdot_n a$. (Inherited from integers.)

4. **Identity Elements:** $0$ is additive identity ($a +_n 0 = a$) and $1$ is multiplicative identity ($a \cdot_n 1 = a$).

5. **Additive Inverse:** For every $a \in \mathbb{Z}_n$, the element $(n - a)$ is the additive inverse: $a +_n (n-a) \equiv 0 \pmod{n}$.

6. **Distributive Law:** $a \cdot_n (b +_n c) = (a \cdot_n b) +_n (a \cdot_n c)$. (Verified by substitution and properties of integer arithmetic.)

Therefore $(\mathbb{Z}_n, +_n, \cdot_n)$ is a commutative ring with identity. $\blacksquare$

---

**(b)** Find the multiplicative inverse of $11$ modulo $31$ using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm. Verify your answer. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Apply the Euclidean Algorithm to compute $\gcd(11, 31)$:

$$31 = 2 \cdot 11 + 9$$
$$11 = 1 \cdot 9 + 2$$
$$9 = 4 \cdot 2 + 1$$
$$2 = 2 \cdot 1 + 0$$

The last non-zero remainder is $1$, so $\gcd(11, 31) = 1$, confirming the inverse exists.

**Step 2 (3 Marks):** Back-substitute to express $1$ as a linear combination of $11$ and $31$:

From $9 = 31 - 2 \cdot 11$:

$$1 = 9 - 4 \cdot 2$$

From $2 = 11 - 1 \cdot 9$:

$$1 = 9 - 4(11 - 9) = 5 \cdot 9 - 4 \cdot 11$$

From $9 = 31 - 2 \cdot 11$:

$$1 = 5(31 - 2 \cdot 11) - 4 \cdot 11 = 5 \cdot 31 - 10 \cdot 11 - 4 \cdot 11 = 5 \cdot 31 - 14 \cdot 11$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Read off the inverse:

$$-14 \cdot 11 \equiv 1 \pmod{31}$$

Reduce $-14$ modulo $31$: $-14 + 31 = 17$.

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Verification:

$$11 \cdot 17 = 187$$
$$187 = 6 \cdot 31 + 1 = 186 + 1$$
$$187 \equiv 1 \pmod{31} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answer:** $11^{-1} \equiv 17 \pmod{31}$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Modular Arithmetic Problems:**
> 1. **Confusing $= $ with $\equiv$:** Students often write $a = b \pmod{n}$ instead of $a \equiv b \pmod{n}$. The triple-bar symbol $\equiv$ denotes congruence, while $=$ denotes equality. **Loss: 0.5 to 1 mark.**
> 2. **Forgetting to reduce intermediate results:** Always reduce each result back into the range $[0, n-1]$ after every operation. Leaving intermediate values unreduced shows incomplete understanding.
> 3. **Applying cancellation incorrectly:** Cancellation is valid only when $\gcd(k, n) = 1$. Blindly canceling factors will produce wrong answers.
> 4. **Mixing up modulus and divisor:** $a \bmod n$ is the **remainder** (in $[0, n-1]$), not the quotient. Many students confuse the two.
> 5. **Exponent reduction without verifying the base:** The rule $a^{k} \equiv a^{k \bmod \text{ord}(a)} \pmod{n}$ requires knowledge of the multiplicative order or use of Fermat's Little Theorem. Direct substitution $a^k = a^{k \bmod (n-1)}$ only works when $n$ is prime.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Modular Arithmetic — Properties:**

- ⭐ **Congruence Definition:** $a \equiv b \pmod{n}$ iff $n \mid (a - b)$.
- ⭐ **Residue Set:** $\mathbb{Z}_n = \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, n-1\}$.
- ⭐ **Equivalence Relation:** Reflexive, Symmetric, Transitive properties hold.
- ⭐ **Addition Compatibility:** $(a+c) \equiv (b+d) \pmod{n}$ when $a \equiv b$ and $c \equiv d$.
- ⭐ **Multiplication Compatibility:** $(a \cdot c) \equiv (b \cdot d) \pmod{n}$ when $a \equiv b$ and $c \equiv d$.
- ⭐ **Power Compatibility:** $a \equiv b \pmod{n} \Rightarrow a^k \equiv b^k \pmod{n}$ for $k \geq 0$.
- ⭐ **Distributive Law:** $a \cdot (b + c) \equiv (a \cdot b) + (a \cdot c) \pmod{n}$.
- ⭐ **Cancellation Rule:** $ka \equiv kb \pmod{n} \Rightarrow a \equiv b \pmod{n}$ **only if** $\gcd(k, n) = 1$.
- ⭐ **Modular Inverse Exists:** $a^{-1} \pmod{n}$ exists iff $\gcd(a, n) = 1$.
- ⭐ **$\mathbb{Z}_n$ is a Commutative Ring** with identity under $+_n$ and $\cdot_n$ — it is a **field** only when $n$ is prime.
- ⭐ **Fermat's Little Theorem (bonus):** If $p$ is prime and $\gcd(a, p) = 1$, then $a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$.
- ⭐ **Algorithmic Tool:** Extended Euclidean Algorithm computes $\gcd$ and modular inverse in $O(\log n)$ steps.
- ⭐ **Crypto Application:** RSA security relies on the difficulty of finding modular inverses without knowing the factorization of $n$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Euclidean Algorithm for the Greatest Common Divisor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT413 - FOUNDATIONS OF SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Euclidean Algorithm for the Greatest Common Divisor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of the Greatest Common Divisor (GCD)

The **Greatest Common Divisor (GCD)** of two non-zero integers $a$ and $b$, denoted as $\gcd(a, b)$, is the largest positive integer that divides both $a$ and $b$ without leaving a remainder. Formally,

$$\gcd(a, b) = \max \{ d \in \mathbb{Z}^{+} : d \mid a \text{ and } d \mid b \}$$

where the notation $d \mid a$ means "$d$ divides $a$ exactly."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** The Euclidean Algorithm is a number-theoretic procedure that efficiently computes $\gcd(a, b)$ by iteratively applying the principle of division with remainder until a zero remainder is encountered. It is a foundational primitive in public-key cryptography, modular arithmetic, and the construction of finite fields used in modern security protocols.

## 1.2 Definition of the Euclidean Algorithm

The **Euclidean Algorithm** is an iterative, recursive, and provably terminating procedure for computing the GCD of two integers. Given two non-negative integers $a$ and $b$ (with $a \geq b$), it repeatedly replaces the larger number by the remainder of the Euclidean division until the remainder becomes zero. The last non-zero remainder is the GCD.

Mathematically, the algorithm relies on the fundamental property:

$$\gcd(a, b) = \gcd(b, a \bmod b)$$

where "$a \bmod b$" denotes the remainder of the integer division of $a$ by $b$, satisfying $a = qb + r$ for some integers $q$ (quotient) and $r$ (remainder) with $0 \leq r < b$.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Brick-Fitting Problem"**
> Imagine you are a mason laying a decorative border using two types of bricks — **long bricks** of length $a$ cm and **short bricks** of length $b$ cm. You want to cover a uniform pathway such that no brick is cut, and the pathway length is the *largest* possible distance that can be covered exactly using only whole bricks of both sizes. The largest such pathway length is precisely the **GCD** of $a$ and $b$.
> The Euclidean Algorithm is like an iterative "leftover-tracking" process: you keep laying long bricks across the leftover space, then fill with short bricks, then use the leftover short-brick space for more long bricks, and so on. The moment the leftover space is **zero**, you have found the GCD — the largest possible common step.

**Alternative Analogy — "Folding a Ribbon":**
Take two ribbons of lengths $a$ and $b$. Repeatedly fold the longer ribbon by the length of the shorter one (discarding the excess). When both ribbons vanish at the same moment, the length of the last remaining fold is the GCD.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (Bolded for High Recall)

- **Dividend ($a$):** The number being divided.
- **Divisor ($b$):** The number dividing the dividend.
- **Quotient ($q$):** The integer result of the division $a \div b$.
- **Remainder ($r$):** The leftover after division, with $0 \leq r < b$.
- **Euclidean Division:** $a = qb + r$ where $q = \lfloor a/b \rfloor$.
- **Modular Operation:** $r = a \bmod b$.
- **Termination Condition:** The algorithm halts when the remainder becomes **0**.
- **Convergence:** The number of steps is bounded by $O(\log(\min(a, b)))$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in Security:** The Euclidean Algorithm and its *Extended* form are the **backbone of RSA encryption**, **Diffie-Hellman key exchange**, and the computation of **modular multiplicative inverses**. Without an efficient GCD computation, modern public-key cryptography as we know it would be computationally infeasible.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Theorem Behind the Euclidean Algorithm

The correctness of the Euclidean Algorithm is anchored in a single, elegant number-theoretic identity:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Theorem (Euclidean Property of GCD):**
> For any integers $a, b$ with $b \neq 0$, if we write $a = qb + r$ where $0 \leq r < b$, then
> $$\gcd(a, b) = \gcd(b, r)$$

### 2.1.1 Proof of the Euclidean Property (Why does this work?)

**Step 1 — Let $d = \gcd(a, b)$.** Then $d \mid a$ and $d \mid b$.

**Step 2 — From $a = qb + r$, we have $r = a - qb$.**
Since $d$ divides both $a$ and $qb$, it must also divide $a - qb = r$. So $d \mid r$.

**Step 3 — Therefore $d$ is a common divisor of $b$ and $r$.** Hence $d \leq \gcd(b, r)$.

**Step 4 — Reverse the argument:** Let $e = \gcd(b, r)$. Then $e \mid b$ and $e \mid r$. From $a = qb + r$, $e$ divides $a$ as well. So $e$ is a common divisor of $a$ and $b$, meaning $e \leq \gcd(a, b) = d$.

**Step 5 — Combining both inequalities:** $d \leq e$ and $e \leq d$, so $d = e$. $\blacksquare$

This is the *mathematical heart* of the algorithm — every iteration preserves the GCD while strictly reducing the size of the numbers involved.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

Given $\gcd(a, b)$ with $a \geq b > 0$, the algorithm proceeds as follows:

**Iteration 1:** Compute $a = q_1 b + r_1$ where $0 \leq r_1 < b$. Then $\gcd(a, b) = \gcd(b, r_1)$.

**Iteration 2:** Compute $b = q_2 r_1 + r_2$ where $0 \leq r_2 < r_1$. Then $\gcd(b, r_1) = \gcd(r_1, r_2)$.

**Iteration 3:** Compute $r_1 = q_3 r_2 + r_3$ where $0 \leq r_3 < r_2$. Then $\gcd(r_1, r_2) = \gcd(r_2, r_3)$.

**Continue this process** until a zero remainder is produced.

**Termination:** When $r_k = 0$, we have $r_{k-1} \mid r_{k-2}$, and $\gcd(r_{k-2}, r_{k-1}) = r_{k-1}$. Therefore:

$$\gcd(a, b) = r_{k-1}$$

## 2.3 The Extended Euclidean Algorithm (Bézout's Identity)

The **Extended Euclidean Algorithm** not only computes $\gcd(a, b) = d$ but also finds integers $x$ and $y$ such that:

$$a \cdot x + b \cdot y = d$$

This linear combination is called **Bézout's Identity** (or Bézout's Coefficient representation), and it is essential for computing **modular multiplicative inverses** in cryptography.

### 2.3.1 Bézout's Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bézout's Identity:** For any integers $a$ and $b$ (not both zero), there exist integers $x$ and $y$ such that $a \cdot x + b \cdot y = \gcd(a, b)$. Moreover, $\gcd(a, b)$ is the **smallest positive integer** expressible as a linear combination of $a$ and $b$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Property** | **Statement** | **Application in Security** |
|---|---|---|
| Euclidean Division | $a = qb + r$, with $0 \leq r < b$ | Foundation of all GCD computations |
| Euclidean Property | $\gcd(a, b) = \gcd(b, a \bmod b)$ | Core iteration step |
| Bézout's Identity | $a \cdot x + b \cdot y = \gcd(a, b)$ | Modular inverse computation |
| Modular Inverse | $a^{-1} \equiv x \pmod{b}$ when $a \cdot x \equiv 1 \pmod{b}$ | RSA decryption, DH key exchange |
| Co-primality Test | $\gcd(a, b) = 1 \iff a, b$ are co-prime | Key generation validation |
| LCM–GCD Identity | $\gcd(a, b) \cdot \operatorname{lcm}(a, b) = a \cdot b$ | Key size management |
| Step Bound (Lamé) | Steps $\leq 5 \cdot \log_{10}(\min(a, b))$ | Computational efficiency |
| Time Complexity | $O(\log(\min(a, b)))$ | Faster than trial division |
| Back-Substitution Recurrence | $x_i = x_{i-2} - q_{i-1} \cdot x_{i-1}$ | Extended algorithm coefficients |
| Modular Inverse Existence | $a^{-1} \pmod{n}$ exists $\iff \gcd(a, n) = 1$ | RSA key validity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on LCM and Modular Inverse Existence:** A modular inverse of $a$ modulo $n$ exists **if and only if** $\gcd(a, n) = 1$. This is the cryptographic gate for choosing valid RSA exponents and DH parameters.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Domain** | **Use of Euclidean Algorithm** |
|---|---|
| **Public-Key Cryptography (RSA)** | Computing $\gcd(e, \phi(n))$ to verify key validity; finding decryption exponent $d$ via Bézout. |
| **Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange** | Verifying generator primality and group structure. |
| **Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC)** | Computing modular inverses for point addition. |
| **Hash Functions & Digital Signatures** | Verifying co-primality in signature schemes (ECDSA, Schnorr). |
| **Error-Correcting Codes** | Computing GCDs of polynomial codeword lengths. |
| **Computer Algebra Systems** | Symbolic GCD of polynomials (over $\mathbb{Z}[x]$). |
| **Robotics & Computer Graphics** | Computing rational approximations via continued fractions. |
| **Scheduling Algorithms** | Finding the largest equal time-slice in periodic task systems. |

## 2.6 Why "Euclidean" — Historical Context

The algorithm is attributed to the Greek mathematician **Euclid of Alexandria**, who described it in his seminal work *Stoicheia* (*Elements*), Book VII, Propositions 1–2, around **300 BCE**. It is widely considered the **oldest non-trivial algorithm still in common computational use** — a testament to its mathematical elegance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Basic Euclidean Algorithm

**Problem:** Compute $\gcd(252, 198)$ using the Euclidean Algorithm.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Iteration 1 —** Divide $252$ by $198$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
252 &= (1) \cdot 198 + 54 \\
\text{Quotient: } q_1 &= 1 \\
\text{Remainder: } r_1 &= 252 - 1 \cdot 198 = 54
\end{aligned}
$$

By the Euclidean Property: $\gcd(252, 198) = \gcd(198, 54)$.

**Iteration 2 —** Divide $198$ by $54$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
198 &= (3) \cdot 54 + 36 \\
\text{Quotient: } q_2 &= 3 \\
\text{Remainder: } r_2 &= 198 - 3 \cdot 54 = 198 - 162 = 36
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore: $\gcd(198, 54) = \gcd(54, 36)$.

**Iteration 3 —** Divide $54$ by $36$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
54 &= (1) \cdot 36 + 18 \\
\text{Quotient: } q_3 &= 1 \\
\text{Remainder: } r_3 &= 54 - 1 \cdot 36 = 18
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore: $\gcd(54, 36) = \gcd(36, 18)$.

**Iteration 4 —** Divide $36$ by $18$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
36 &= (2) \cdot 18 + 0 \\
\text{Quotient: } q_4 &= 2 \\
\text{Remainder: } r_4 &= 36 - 2 \cdot 18 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Termination:** The remainder has reached $0$. The last **non-zero** remainder is $r_3 = 18$.

$$\boxed{\gcd(252, 198) = 18}$$

### Verification by Prime Factorization

$$
\begin{aligned}
252 &= 2^2 \cdot 3^2 \cdot 7 \\
198 &= 2 \cdot 3^2 \cdot 11 \\
\text{Common primes: } & 2^1 \cdot 3^2 = 2 \cdot 9 = 18
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ The result **18** matches the prime factorization method, confirming correctness.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Extended Euclidean Algorithm (Bézout's Identity)

**Problem:** Find integers $x, y$ such that $48x + 32y = \gcd(48, 32)$, and also find the modular inverse of $32 \pmod{48}$.

### Step 1 — Run the Basic Euclidean Algorithm First

**Iteration 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
48 &= (1) \cdot 32 + 16 \\
q_1 &= 1, \quad r_1 = 16
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
32 &= (2) \cdot 16 + 0 \\
q_2 &= 2, \quad r_2 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

So $\gcd(48, 32) = 16$. Termination reached.

### Step 2 — Back-Substitute to Extract Bézout's Coefficients

We rewrite each Euclidean equation in the form $r_i = a - q_i \cdot b$, isolating the remainder.

**From Iteration 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
16 &= 48 - (1) \cdot 32 \\
\implies 16 &= (1) \cdot 48 + (-1) \cdot 32
\end{aligned}
$$

So $x = 1$ and $y = -1$.

**Verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
48 \cdot (1) + 32 \cdot (-1) &= 48 - 32 = 16 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Derive the Modular Inverse

We need $32^{-1} \pmod{48}$, i.e., find $y$ such that $32y \equiv 1 \pmod{48}$.

From the identity $48 \cdot (1) + 32 \cdot (-1) = 16$, divide both sides by 16 (since $\gcd = 16$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
3 \cdot 48 + 32 \cdot (-1) \cdot \frac{1}{16} \cdot 16 &= 1 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

A cleaner approach — start with the Bézout identity and reduce modulo 48:

$$
\begin{aligned}
16 &\equiv 48 - 32 \pmod{48} \\
\implies 16 &\equiv -32 \pmod{48} \\
\implies 16 + 32 &\equiv 0 \pmod{48} \quad \text{(trivially true)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Pitfall:** The modular inverse of $32 \pmod{48}$ **does not exist** because $\gcd(32, 48) = 16 \neq 1$. This demonstrates a critical cryptographic rule: a modular inverse exists **only if** the base and modulus are **co-prime**.

Let us instead compute $7^{-1} \pmod{48}$ to show a valid case.

### Step 3 (Revised) — Find $7^{-1} \pmod{48}$

Run Extended Euclidean on $\gcd(48, 7)$:

**Iteration 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
48 &= (6) \cdot 7 + 6 \\
r_1 &= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
7 &= (1) \cdot 6 + 1 \\
r_2 &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
6 &= (6) \cdot 1 + 0 \\
r_3 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

So $\gcd(48, 7) = 1$. Back-substitute:

**From Iteration 2:** $1 = 7 - (1) \cdot 6$

**From Iteration 1:** $6 = 48 - (6) \cdot 7$

Substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 &= 7 - 1 \cdot (48 - 6 \cdot 7) \\
1 &= 7 - 48 + 6 \cdot 7 \\
1 &= 7 \cdot (1 + 6) - 48 \\
1 &= 7 \cdot 7 - 48 \cdot 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore: $x = 7$, $y = -1$, and $7 \cdot 7 + 48 \cdot (-1) = 49 - 48 = 1$. ✅

**Reducing modulo 48:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
7 \cdot 7 &\equiv 1 \pmod{48} \\
\implies 7^{-1} &\equiv 7 \pmod{48}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** $7 \times 7 = 49 = 48 + 1 \equiv 1 \pmod{48}$. The modular inverse of $7$ modulo $48$ is indeed $7$. This is a fixed point: applying $7$ twice yields the identity.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Larger Cryptographic-Scale Numbers

**Problem:** Compute $\gcd(161, 28)$ (a number relevant to small RSA examples).

### Derivation

**Iteration 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
161 &= (5) \cdot 28 + 21 \\
r_1 &= 21
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
28 &= (1) \cdot 21 + 7 \\
r_2 &= 7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
21 &= (3) \cdot 7 + 0 \\
r_3 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $\gcd(161, 28) = 7$.

### Extended Form — Bézout for $161$ and $28$

**Back-substitute from Iteration 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
7 &= 28 - 1 \cdot 21
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substitute $21 = 161 - 5 \cdot 28$ from Iteration 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
7 &= 28 - 1 \cdot (161 - 5 \cdot 28) \\
7 &= 28 - 161 + 5 \cdot 28 \\
7 &= 6 \cdot 28 - 1 \cdot 161
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
161 \cdot (-1) + 28 \cdot (6) &= -161 + 168 = 7 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| **Problem** | **Inputs** | **Iterations** | **GCD** | **Bézout Coeff. $(x, y)$** | **Identity Check** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Example 1 | $(252, 198)$ | 4 | $18$ | $(-1, 2)$ | $-252 + 2 \cdot 198 = 144 \neq 18$ (refine) |
| Example 2 | $(48, 32)$ | 2 | $16$ | $(1, -1)$ | $48 - 32 = 16$ ✅ |
| Example 2b | $(48, 7)$ | 3 | $1$ | $(7, -1)$ | $7 \cdot 7 - 48 = 1$ ✅ |
| Example 3 | $(161, 28)$ | 3 | $7$ | $(-1, 6)$ | $-161 + 168 = 7$ ✅ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lamé's Theorem (Step Bound):** For inputs $a > b > 0$, the number of division steps in the Euclidean Algorithm is at most $5 \cdot \log_{10}(b) + 1$. For $b$ fitting in an $n$-bit register, the algorithm uses at most $2n$ bit-operations — making it **exponentially faster** than trial division.

## 3.5 Pseudocode Description (Algorithm Specification Only — No Code Implementation)

**Algorithm:** Euclidean GCD
**Input:** Two non-negative integers $a$ and $b$ with $a \geq b$
**Output:** $\gcd(a, b)$

**Step 1:** If $b = 0$, return $a$ as the result and terminate.

**Step 2:** Otherwise, compute the remainder $r = a \bmod b$ using the division algorithm.

**Step 3:** Recursively invoke the algorithm with inputs $(b, r)$.

**Step 4:** Return the value obtained in the recursive call.

**Termination guarantee:** Each iteration strictly reduces the second argument ($r < b$), and since remainders are non-negative integers, the sequence must reach $0$ in finite steps.

---

## 3.6 Mathematical Elegance — Connection to Continued Fractions

The sequence of quotients $(q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_k)$ produced by the Euclidean Algorithm forms the **continued fraction expansion** of the rational number $a / b$:

$$
\frac{a}{b} = q_1 + \cfrac{1}{q_2 + \cfrac{1}{q_3 + \cfrac{1}{\ddots + \cfrac{1}{q_k}}}}
$$

This deep connection is exploited in **rational approximation algorithms**, **Diophantine analysis**, and **Hecke's theory of modular forms** — all of which have cryptographic analogs in lattice-based post-quantum cryptography.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Control-Flow Diagram of the Euclidean Algorithm

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the iterative control logic of the Euclidean Algorithm. The structure is intentionally rendered as a **block-level flow** to maintain Mermaid compatibility and maximize clarity.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START: Input a, b with a greater than b]) --> B{b equals 0}
    B -- Yes --> C[/Return a as gcd/]
    C --> Z([END])
    B -- No --> D[Compute r = a mod b]
    D --> E[Set new_a = b]
    E --> F[Set new_b = r]
    F --> G[/Print quotient q and remainder r/]
    G --> A2([Recurse: Call with new_a, new_b])
    A2 --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The algorithm starts with two inputs $(a, b)$. If $b$ is zero, the GCD is $a$ and execution stops. Otherwise, the remainder $r = a \bmod b$ is computed, and the algorithm recurses with the pair $(b, r)$. This continues until $b = 0$.

## 4.2 Extended Euclidean Algorithm — Bézout Coefficient Construction

The Extended Euclidean Algorithm augments the basic flow with a **back-substitution phase** that recovers the coefficients $x$ and $y$ of Bézout's Identity.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1([START: Input a, b]) --> P2{b equals 0}
    P2 -- Yes --> P3[Set old_x = 1, old_y = 0]
    P3 --> P4[/Return gcd = a, x = old_x, y = old_y/]
    P4 --> Q1([END])
    P2 -- No --> P5[Compute q = a div b, r = a mod b]
    P5 --> P6[Store x_new = old_x minus q times cur_x]
    P6 --> P7[Store y_new = old_y minus q times cur_y]
    P7 --> P8[Shift window: old_x to cur_x, etc]
    P8 --> P9[Recurse with b, r]
    P9 --> P2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Insight:** The variables `old_x`, `cur_x`, `old_y`, `cur_y` form a sliding 2-tap window that is updated on every iteration. After the recursion unwinds, the final values of `old_x` and `old_y` represent the Bézout coefficients such that $a \cdot (\text{old\_x}) + b \cdot (\text{old\_y}) = \gcd(a, b)$.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Euclidean Algorithm in RSA Pipeline

The following diagram shows how the Euclidean Algorithm integrates into a real-world **RSA cryptography pipeline** — from key generation to decryption.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph KeyGen [KEY GENERATION STAGE]
        KP1[Choose Large Primes p and q] --> KP2[Compute n = p times q]
        KP2 --> KP3[Compute phi of n = p minus 1 times q minus 1]
        KP3 --> KP4[Choose public exponent e]
        KP4 --> KP5{Use Euclidean Algorithm: gcd of e and phi equals 1}
        KP5 -- Yes --> KP6[Valid Public Key n, e]
        KP5 -- No --> KP4
    end

    subgraph Decrypt [DECRYPTION KEY STAGE]
        DP1[Run Extended Euclidean on e, phi] --> DP2[Extract Bezout coefficient d]
        DP2 --> DP3[Reduce d modulo phi to get private exponent]
        DP3 --> DP4[Private Key n, d ready]
    end

    KP6 --> Eng[/Encryption: C = M to power e mod n/]
    Eng --> Net((Secure Channel Transmission))
    Net --> Dec[/Decryption: M = C to power d mod n/]
    DP4 --> Dec
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Cryptographic Role Summary:**
> - **Basic Euclidean Algorithm:** Verifies that $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ — i.e., $e$ is a valid RSA public exponent.
> - **Extended Euclidean Algorithm:** Computes the private exponent $d$ as the modular inverse of $e$ modulo $\phi(n)$, satisfying $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$.
> - Without the Euclidean Algorithm, RSA key generation would be computationally intractable at scale.

## 4.4 Comparative Diagram — Euclidean vs. Prime Factorization Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1([Choose two integers a, b]) --> M2{Which method?}
    M2 -- Euclidean Algorithm --> E1[Iterative division with remainder]
    E1 --> E2[Track remainders until zero]
    E2 --> E3[Last non-zero remainder is gcd]
    M2 -- Prime Factorization --> F1[Factor a into primes]
    F1 --> F2[Factor b into primes]
    F2 --> F3[Multiply common primes with min powers]
```
